Alright, now we're going to get into one of my favorite parts, which is hooks. Now what is a hook? You probably have heard guys talking about hooks, especially copywriters. A hook is an instantly arresting word or phrase or visual or an idea that grabs your reader and allows you to reel him into your message. It's very much like the hook on a fishing line. The hooks are the heartbeat of a great sales message. The best ones are very memorable. They're the kind of line that the reader has to stop everything else they're doing and sit down and pay attention to what you've presented to them. They're either going to read it, they're going to watch it if it's a video, they're going to listen to it if it's audio. And it's kind of a wake up call too. As we call it, it's the best thing that happened to him today or the oddest thing that's happened to him during the day. It is something unique in his day. Again, most people read or watch videos in a passive state. Your job when you're creating a sales message is to bring the reader out of the passive state into an active state so that you can get him to do something. Pick up the phone and call, start clicking on the mouse and going to League, start signing up for things. It's action. It's not passive. We'll get into this in just a second. So when you're hunting for hooks, when you're looking for the hook that will sometimes be in the headline or sometimes be in the theme of the ad that you're writing, what you want to do is if you're a freelancer, you will interview your client and if you're writing for yourself, you're going to have to interview yourself. You're going to have to dig deep and you're going to have to get out of your own head and start looking at things in a unique way. We'll explore how to do that as we go. Top writers always think in terms of hooks. For headlines, email subject lines, testimonials, speeches, even when they're standing in front of someone doing a verbal sales pitch or they're just, anytime you need to persuade someone to do something that they are not otherwise inclined to do, a hook is going to help you break through the white noise in your prospects head. Now you have to go deep. Keep asking yourself, keep asking your client, keep asking whoever is the subject of the matter you're writing about, keep asking the why, when, where, what, who and how stuff. That comes from journalism. Answering those questions, why is it this way, when did this happen, where did the things occur, who was involved, how did it transpire. You want to sniff out the hidden back stories. Now the whole idea is to bring the reader out of his world and into your world. His world often will be, again, full of white noise, kind of humdrum. It's the same old day that he's having, which is full of problems, full of situations, which whatever you are offering is going to help fix, is going to make better. So your world is different than his. Your world is one where things work, where things go smoothly, where problems get solved, where solutions are found. So you're going to bring him into your world. Now the company line is what your client will tell you is the reason that people buy from him or why they're interested in what he offers. The company line is usually wrong. It's what they put in their pamphlets, it's what they have on their business cards, it's slogans they'll toss around very casually. They are not created by salesmen. You being a salesman now are going to be able to find out what really moves people. And this is why instead of going with the company line to search for hooks, we look into the gossip around the product. We look into opinions, rumor, common wisdom. We want to find out what the competition says about themselves and says about you and what your rank and file says about the competition. You want to get into the myths, the legends, complaints from customers and true tales from people who are actually using the product or service or people that are actually doing customer service. This means that when you are looking for stuff, if your freelance are working with a client, you can ask the client all of the stuff that he thinks may be a hook or actually don't ask him for a hook, he won't even know what you're talking about. But you can search for the hook through the official stuff, the brochures and the material and all the stuff that the top guys or the top women in the business say is, again, the reason that people are buying from them or are part of their world. But you really want to talk to what we call the feet in the street. If they have salesmen who actually deal with customers and you want to talk to them and you want to find out what the customers are saying about why they deal with the company, good and bad. You want to get opinions from people. I mean, a lot of people will not tell you their opinions when you're interviewing them, so you've got to push for that. Because even if you don't use a lot of this stuff, you want to find out what they really think, not what they think the company line is. Rumors are great. I've gotten a lot of hooks just from talking to people outside of the company who said, well, wait, that company does X or that client was known for doing X when he was in college or whatever. It might not use it, it might use it. You will see some examples. I'm going to give you some examples of hooks right after we talk about these specific details. The key, as you will see, is the incongruous juxtaposition of compelling sales elements. Now I'll bring that phrase up again when we're talking about some of the headlines that I'm about to show you. But think about this, the incongruous juxtaposition of compelling sales elements. That's a phrase I came up with, I don't know, 15, 20 years ago. It gets smiles all the time from professionals in advertising, but they know exactly what I'm talking about. It's the idea of balancing what seem like opposing thoughts within a headline or a phrase or using words that don't seem to fit, but yet create a storyline or actually bring the reader into your tail as you're going through your sales message. Just a few more secrets before I get into the examples. Your job is to wake up the scenario blob welded to the couch. I like to think of my potential prospect as a half asleep blob of a human being, a total couch potato who wouldn't get up and leave the building if it was on fire. You have to write something that he either reads or hears or watches that gets him so excited that he's going to move, that he's going to get up, he's going to go get his wallet, he's going to pick up the phone, he's going to start clicking on links that you want him to click on. That's the job in front of you. That's why advertising can be so difficult because you're dealing with a prospect who isn't naturally inclined to say, yeah, okay, you want me to buy that? I'll buy it. One of the ways we do this is you want to be the one thing he reads today that gets his blood moving. That means he does that startle thing that you see in movies a lot where they just go what? Or they go, oh my God, this is what I've been looking for? Or wow, that's interesting. Where materiality comes, must read, must hear, must watch. Remember that most people lead lives of quiet desperation. They don't have exciting mornings. They're not going to an exciting, wonderful job. They don't have a lot of nice things to look forward to in the hours ahead of them. They're just going to plod through their day. If their day is also riddled with problems, with situations that they'd like to change, but they don't have the first idea of how to change it or how to fix it or how to even approach the problems, and you come along and you have solutions and you have ways to deal with things, then you become, to them, to the perfect prospect, you become the best thing that they've read today because their life changes. I've actually used that in ads. Remember today, what happened before was your old life. What's going to happen after today when you've discovered this is going to be completely different and more wonderful and different things are going to happen to you. And a lot of people respond to that. Your big advantage is this, in fact. His life is mostly ho-hum. Your competition certainly usually isn't helping the situation either. They're doing boring ads. They're doing long pedantic messages that just bounce off of their brain. So you want to be that message that makes his day. And you'll see how these examples make that manifest. So one of the problems, again, you're up against, is that all sales involve an inherently hostile interaction between the advertiser and the prospect. You and your prospect are not on the same page until you make it happen. So they are not inclined to agree with you, no matter what you say. Even if you say, I have the absolute solution to all of your problems in life and it's cheap and easy and fast and we can get started right now. Even if you can make that claim legitimately and ethically, you still won't get the perfect prospect to say, yeah, okay, fine, let's do it. They have a built up resistance to being sold, to all advertising, to all incoming material. They just have to be a little bit cynical and they really don't want to be in a position where they want what you have. Because that changes thing. That puts a different spin on their day. They now have desires. They now have to follow through. What they thought they were going to do in the next few minutes is now going to change because they're now going to read or watch or listen to what you presented with them. They know it's taking them off of their normal schedule and they're going somewhere else and all of this presents things that maybe they would rather not do. So you have to be aware of that at all times. In fact, the first thing that he wants to do after you've grabbed a prospect's attention is to find an excuse not to engage. He's going to hunt for reasons why I can put this off or I don't need to look at this now or it's not for me or I would never be able to afford this. He's got all these objections suddenly start stirring up in his head and swirling around and they're all meant to get him out of that situation where he's paying attention to you. Hooks rivet his attention because he's got to find out what's going on. The new problem that you have presented, which is if you change, if you follow me, if you use the solution I have or you take the actions that I'm suggesting or my services are something you decide to use, then things are going to change and people are resistant to change. So the hook keeps him riveted until you can make your case. And finally, you never know what a hook is before you find it. A lot of writers pretend to sit down and say, I'm going to write a hook or I'm going to figure out what the hook is. You have to look for it. You can't decide beforehand what the hook's going to be. Hooks are things that surprise you and they come from looking for them. And I'll explain the history of how some of the hooks came up that we're about to look at. You should study good hooks. Take note of the bad ones too and avoid the traps and we'll talk about that in a second. So one of the good ways to start, line up the best objections and how you counter them. In a face-to-face conversation, what are the usual objections that a prospect might have to what is in your sales message? It's too expensive. I don't have the time. This isn't right for me. Just get down into it. And then how do you counter them? How do you quickly say, well, no, there's a payment plan. So it's not too expensive. And it's a lot cheaper than going along doing what you were doing anyway because that's costing you a lot of money. It's very, very easy. You don't need a college education. In fact, you don't need to understand anything at all about anything. You don't need to understand code or technology or anything. All of that's done for you. What are the ways that you counter the objections? Line all that up because sometimes the hook will be in this particular area. Do not expect logic or rational thought in your reader. One of the worst kind of hooks that people come up with are totally logical and rational hooks. But you have to understand that the average reader is only looking to reinforce his already existing belief systems. And a lot of people think, I never buy from ads. I've encountered this with a number of friends and colleagues in other businesses where they say, well, I don't buy from ads myself. And I will look around their house. And it's full of Chotsky's and stuff that they bought from direct response ads, either on late night television infomercials or they buy from print ads or they respond to banner ads. So in their belief system, they don't believe they've ever bought from an ad when, in fact, almost everything they own has been triggered by seeing some kind of advertising or some message given to them. So this kind of disconnect is what you're dealing with. So be thinking about this. And you definitely want to identify power words and key phrases as you edit your hooks. One word can dramatically change everything. And you have the power words report. So referring to that is often a good way to get your mind working on different words to use and I'll identify some of the words in the coming examples, which we're almost into. And again, understand how to swipe good hooks. It requires a deaf touch. One of the ads I'm going to show you is a very famous and notorious one that I wrote early on for my golf client, which was about a one-legged golfer. And I'll show you that ad in a minute. But I kept seeing a lot of people trying to rip that ad and they came up with the one-legged accountant, which doesn't work because a one-legged golfer is one of those what? How does a guy golf if he's only got one leg? Well, one-legged accountant, it doesn't affect the job. So there's no connection there. There's no incongruous juxtaposition there. So you've got to be aware of what matters, what gives that wow response, and what is just a yeah, so what kind of thing. So positioning matters. Genius versus fan versus duffer in golf, for example. And here's the first headline I want to do. How a skinny little golf genius from California accidentally started hitting 425-yard tee shots. Now for the non-golfers out there, a average duffer, and a duffer is a word for the average golfer, which is not a very good golfer. The average drive is probably what? 100 to 125 yards and sometimes less than that. The pros, of course, will hit 300-yard drives during PGA competitions. So when you talk about 425 yards to an audience that understands golf, they know that 425 is not just long, it's beyond long. It's almost unimaginably long for a tee shot. So this 425-yard tee shot coupled with a skinny little golf genius. And for most people out there, especially in the hinterlands of America, genius is not necessarily a trusted term. In America, we rightly so, I think, tend to mistrust people who refer to themselves as geniuses. We're not sure what their motivations are. There's a lot of history packed into the word genius. However, a golf genius and skinny little. So you have in your mind, this starts to tell the story, from California that also kind of starts to deepen the story a little bit. Maybe from California isn't that important, but it fills out the headline. The key word here, if you haven't guessed yet, is accidentally. Because how does a skinny little guy in a game where a 200-yard drive is extraordinary, hit a 425-yard tee shot accidentally? And of course, it's not shot, it's shots. So this guy, he is now hitting 425-yard tee shots from an accident, and he's a skinny little guy. This is a hook. This is an example for an average golfer who's going to say, wow, this is kind of a wake-up thing, I've got to find out what's going on. This then becomes must read. This is also from the golf client that I had. I have a number of these from the golf client again, because that client took the leash off me. He really let me go deep with hooks, and even when they were nervous about some of the ads that I ran, they swore they would run everything as I wrote it and take the heat afterwards. And of course, instead of the heat, they wound up making a lot of money, and a lot of these ads are still running even 20 years later. They have survived being in direct mail long ago. They easily transferred to the web, and they become VSL video sales letters that are still running today. So the power of a good hook is pretty remarkable. So your brain is trying to kill your golf game. The key word there, of course, is kill. The idea of your brain trying to kill your golf game, this doesn't quite make sense. And the product was an instructional about how the visual perception of the golf ball when you're standing over it, because of the way you do it, was actually kind of cockeyed for people. And if you did a few things to adjust the way you actually looked at the way you swung, you actually would make better contact with the ball. And this was a very good product. But the regular headline would have been how to hit the ball longer by correcting your visual adjustments. I mean, it would be very boring. It wouldn't mean much, and people would not be interested. However, to start with, your brain is trying to kill your golf game. This presents the kind of story that people want to read. This is the one leg of the golfer ad I was referring to. The amazing secret discovered by one-legged golfer adds 50 yards to your drives, eliminates hooks and slices, and can slash up to 10 strokes from your game almost overnight. So this is a secret discovered by a one-legged golfer. The almost overnight, you're slashing 10 strokes. 10 strokes is a lot. A lot of golfers golf fairly consistently, and 10 strokes off their game is going to mean a lot to them. So the story of how I got to this is pretty illuminating. I was given the job by this client who wanted me to interview a, what we call a talent, a golfer who had a certain swing that they were going to market as an information product. It was going to be how to learn this guy's swing and it would increase your game. It would add yards to your drives, eliminate hooks and slices, and make your overall game very good. However, it was pretty boring. It was pretty mundane. New swings are a dime a dozen. So I started talking to the guy and I finally said after about 45 minutes into a very long interview, where did you come up with the idea for your swing, for your new swing? He says, that's an old story. Nobody wants to hear that. And I said, well, tell me anyway. He says, okay, I was golfing one time and we're behind a foursome. And one of the guys has only had one leg. And so in the first tee is we're waiting for them to tee off. I watched this guy hop up to the tee and he started swinging. I thought for sure he was going to fall over, but he didn't. He hit this gorgeous straight tee shot and then got in the golf cart and took off and continued with his game. And I had an epiphany. The epiphany was the balance secrets that the one-legged golfer had would work just as well for a golfer with two legs. And this is how this swing developed. And I said, that's a great hook. Why don't you want to tell that anymore? And he says, ah, my wife's tired of hearing it. My kids are tired of hearing it. All my buddies don't want to hear it anymore. So for him, it was a boring old story that didn't mean anything. For me, as soon as I heard it, it became the hook that went into one of the bestselling golf ads that my client ever had. And so it still gets referred to by a lot of people. This is now an iconic ad in the advertising industry. It always gets a chuckle and a smile, but people read it and people are very, very interested in how this one-legged golfer was able to accomplish all of this. Now again, a lot of people take this ad and they think, well, how about if I just replace golfer with, you know, accountant, lawyer, plumber, whatever. And it isn't the same thing. So a blind accountant may matter, but things like an overweight doctor helps people lose weight almost overnight. That would be an example of a hook. Not a well-educated Harvard-trained doctor helps people lose weight. That has no catch to it. This is a good one. The naked girls all laughed behind the little pudgy guys back until he got in a knife fight with three enormous badass bikers. Now this was an instructional DVD series on self-defense. And it was based on a guy who essentially had a job as a bouncer. He was kind of small. He was a little overweight. And he got jobs at the worst bars in town and did very, very well. One of the jobs he had was at a strip bar. And as I was interviewing him, this became the hook, the idea of naked girls laughing behind this guy's back until he got into a knife fight with three enormous bikers. And there the juxtaposition of that, the idea of the small guy who's being laughed at and you know you're in for a rollicking tale. Here's one for a piece I wrote for Rodale, one of the largest direct mail outfits in the world. And it was for a book where it was just they interviewed doctors and nurses and sexologists. And it was a very straightforward, somewhat conservative book about better sex. But the headline of the original headline, which was going to be something like, you know, boring conservative book about sex tells you everything you wanted to know. I went deeper and I found some of the things that were in the book were about women talking about their side of things, which a lot of men were clueless about and had never copped to. So the headline, what women secretly wish you knew about sex but will never, ever tell you to your face. So that becomes a hook for males. And this was a book aimed at men. So this hook was quite successful. And now here's a series of some golf ads. So this was a good one too. This was, I interviewed the guy and this was a guy who was a very good golfer and existed on a golfing, in a golfing world that kind of went beneath the radar of the media. So even though he was playing PGA pros in private games, this was for cash money. So it wasn't for tournaments. It wasn't reported in the news. This was, again, private games. And as I interviewed him, it was very, very interesting what I found out, which naturally went into the hook. That became the headline and some of the copy. So this guy fits all of this. So the headline is, How Does an Out of Shape 55-Year-Old Golfer Crippled by Arthritis and 71 Pounds Over Weight Still Consistently Humiliate PGA Pros in Head-to-Head Matches by Hitting Every T-Shot Farther and Straighter Down the Fairway. It's funny, it says further should be farther, of course. But little mistakes like that don't hurt in an ad. So this guy was out of weight, overweight. He was way out of shape. But the idea of consistently humiliating PGA pros wasn't just beating them. He was humiliating them. And humiliate is one of those power words that carries some emotional impact. Everybody's been humiliated in their lives. People understand that. It is a very, very powerful word that can carry a story, just a single word. Now here's kind of a good part of this. Darrell, you see, is pushing 60 easily 70 pounds overweight, so nearsighted his glasses look like Coke bottle bottoms. And he has advanced osteoarthritis, so serious. The doctors want to replace his knees with slabs of plastic right now. You hurt just watching him hobble up to the tee and wonder, how the heck is this poor, old, pathetic guy going to swing a golf club? And of course he hits the ball straight and true and has the secrets for other people who are not athletic, young, well-trained golfers. So for the average golfer, which I've explained before, is generally not in good shape. He's a little older. He doesn't look anything like the professionals you see on TV. He doesn't have a windmill swing. This guy becomes a hero. So that's a good example of finding by interviewing the guy. Now I asked him once he told me all this, I said, is it okay if I put all of this into the ad? He says, yeah, whatever works. So he was a perfect client. However, some clients may resist this stuff, but you've got to work from strength. So I want to push it as far as I can using the truth. But sometimes you're lucky and you find a client who says, yes, this will work. Let's do it. Now going on in this series of ads, I was doing a lot of golf ads for this client and they actually were finding some guys with really interesting stories. So this story was crippled golfer stuns LA Open Crowd by crawling from deathbed to beat 110 pro golfers with a 67 using a short backswing and qualify for the last spot in the famous 2002 tournament. So the story was, I interviewed this guy and the story that he was initially given to me was that, yeah, no one expected him to get into the LA Open, but the Los Angeles Open. But he got into it and he got one of the last spots and I kept pushing, well, what's the story? He was in bed, he had, things were very, very bad and the docs really didn't want him to go and work, but this was something that he needed to do and he did it. He went out of the hospital against doctors' orders and went down and actually had a great game and the secrets that he used to be able to have a great game even under adverse circumstances became the meat of the story to be able to sell his teaching program. So going on in this vein, this is another golfer that this client found me who, I've just got to read it, how a blind golfer stumbled onto the simple secret behind David Duvall's swing that can give anyone unbelievable distance with total accuracy. There is a group of blind golfers in the country who actually, they will close the course and they go out and they have people who will help them, but they love the game so much that they golf and they are legally blind. So this guy was actually part of this group, this association of blind golfers and the story just kind of wrote itself. And that's a good start for yourself to start getting a good idea of what high-end hooks look like. Some of the hooks you come up with will be a little less dramatic, but they are so important because you're battling against the senambulant blob welded to the couch who really doesn't want to change even if he has severe problems, who is really not inclined to follow your suggestions and you have to kind of wake him up and be that thing that happens in his day where he says, yes, I'm going to come along and see what's going on. So let's go to the next subject. All right, hooks. I've called some questions over the years. You'll see some of these are really about hooks and some of them are, they start coming at this point of view, so I thought let's get to them and answer people's questions who have them out there. Hooks versus headlines. A lot of people say, this seems like most of the examples you used or I'm thinking things I see as headlines or openings of videos. So are hooks always headlines or headlines, hooks the same thing? No, but hooks naturally lend themselves to good headlines. In the copy that I showed in the one about the guy who was 70 pounds overweight and humiliated PGA golfers consistently, there's hooks inside of that too, inside of the copy and an ad that we used in a previous thing about riding buck naked on a clapped out two stroke. That is a hook, for example. So hooks wind up anywhere. Hooks wind up in subject lines of emails, for example. Hooks can wind up in the titles of a book or chapter headings of a book or the main theme of a speech that you give if you talk in front of audiences anywhere or themes in seminars or just come up regularly in VSL video sales letters. When you have a lot of hooks, you're sitting on a bag of wealth because sometimes people only come up with one hook and then they play off of that and that's pretty much the gist of the piece. If you can have multiple hooks in there, you're just reawakening the reader or the viewer as you go. More is better. Okay, that makes sense. The answer is obviously yes, your headline will be a hook in the opening, but it's certainly it's not the same thing. There's many places to use hooks as you just rattled off several examples. Something that you taught me years ago that I initially, I pushed back on, but I've now seen is absolutely a good process. So I'd like you to talk a little bit about that, is when you're formulating these ideas in your own sketch pad before you showed anybody, you always said it's easier to dial back than it is to raise the claim later. So just talk a little bit about that. Really good point. A lot of top writers will write starting with the most outrageous thing they can come up with, knowing that the first early drafts will not be the drafts that wind up being the sales message or the ad or the email or the VSL script. It's easier to start being really outrageous and going off the rails and then dial it back as we say, say, I can't say that or modify this or do that and start to winnow it back to a more reasonable language, a more reasonable story. But it's a lot harder to start with a weak message and then try to beef that up, try to build that up. That's tough to do because you're building on top of a shaky, weak foundation. So start with a really strong thing. Just go balls to the wall right off the bat and write the strongest, most outrageous, crazy throw in everything you can, writing the most perfect piece you could in another universe. You could get away with swearing, with making grandiose statements and doing all this and then dial it back to a more reasonable way of presenting your message. So yeah, a lot of writers start off being totally off the wall and then they dial it back. It's a really good tactic for all writers of all levels. Yeah, because I've seen that myself where the draft that no one but me is going to see, it just while you're just sort of at this point actually working on the story. The exercises up to now have been mostly, we're building the war chest, the ideas that we're now with hooks, you're actually word smithing now. We're starting to write for the first time, not just collect ideas. And yeah, so that first draft, when you're just throwing ideas around, you're hitting the golf ball 800 yards. You never have to work again and your stock portfolio will double every minute. Then clearly you're not, that's not what your product does, but from that story, you then, okay, because you and I have this process among ourselves. You'll frequently show me the draft and my role in that, oh, we get, no, we can't say this, or no, I talk to the client too. No, that's where, but they say it's much more effective than we have to take that back to reality, but if we started understanding, you never get to the excitement. So would it be fair to say, again, I'm definitely stealing a quote from another question, you're looking for the most outrageous, unbelievable, but true thing with the hook. The one-legged golfer to me is sort of like, there's a whole marketing lesson in that one hook. Yes, that's a good example of with normal clients or with most clients, that wouldn't have made it to the final piece, because the client would say, we can't say that, and that's too outrageous. Nobody's going to buy that, but it turns out that this client agreed to take the leash off, which meant that they said, as much as we're nervous about stuff, we will let you make the decision, me, the writer, and they will run with what I write. And that was a good example of when they were nervous, and this ran as a four-page ad in major golf magazines, quite a bit of outlay of cash to be able to get that. And so they were nervous, they lost a lot of sleep waiting for that to run, and it was a huge hit right off the bat. In fact, the agent at the magazine begged my client not to run that ad. He said, you're going to ruin your reputation, nobody's going to read this, you're going to be the laughing stock of the market, and they just said, Carlton said, we should do it, so we're going to do it. And they did it, and it started to change the way the magazine dealt with ads. They tried to hide the ad because the ad kept running, and they kept begging them not to run the ad because they thought they were wasting their money. They thought they were doing my client a favor, why begging them not to run that ad? But they kept reinserting the ad issue after issue. So they started catching on, and it was working, so they tried to move it around. They moved it to the back of the magazine. They tried to do everything they could to prove themselves right, which some people will do. And it ran for a very, very long time. And it's a good example of how once you understand what goes into a good direct response sales message, you now know more than a lot of people who claim to be experts, such as people in ad agencies or PR agencies or people who run magazines. Who think they know what people want in their advertising, and they will try to talk you out of certain things. So starting with the outrageous thing, I don't remember what the original headlines were for that, but they were even more outrageous than that. I dialed it back a bit, but like I say, with an average client, I would have had to keep dialing it back until it got pretty mushy and ineffective. So you begin to develop after some experience an idea of what your market wants to hear. And I know bad golfers, or I have been a regular average golfer my entire life. And I know what they believe, what they want to hear, what delights them, what frustrates them. And this is something that a lot of the professional golfers do not understand. Yeah, that's interesting because a good example of something we've talked about several times, that you are not your target market. You have solved this problem, thus you can lose sight. The professional golfers have a frequently long lost sight of the struggling average person because they are not swimming in that pool anymore. I love that one leg golf, for example. I think there's a whole mini masters of marketing there. Speaking of events, I've asked people, you showed the headline talk about it, it's like if I raise your hands, who just think this is funny, ridiculous? The thing is, once you identify in that room of hundreds or thousands of people, the golfers, and then how many of the golfers, you still want to know more about this, it's a phenomenal percentage of their hands are still in the air. While the non-golfers are just, that's ridiculous, that would never work in my market. Really good point. Yeah, that's the break people make. Clearly, if you're not interested in golfing, you might have chuckled and turned the page. But if you are interested in golfing and are interested in improving your game, you almost have to at least scan down to, at least read the bold print, see the highlights there. Okay, we've had people from all over the world take the course. Really common question comes up at this point. Yeah, you Americans fall for this stuff. We fill in every other country on planet Earth. We don't go for that. We don't respond to ads like this, et cetera, et cetera. So how do we help those people? Well, this is a larger problem in business where everybody thinks their audience is different. My clients are different. My customers are different. My prospects are different. And I will tell you, after 30 years in the business, that ain't true. It's just absolutely not true. In fact, Sam, you have a personal insight into this. You spent a lot of time in Holland. Yeah, one of the stories we, I spent a lot of time in Europe. I used to work frequently there. I'd go there on holiday. And you Americans do this and obviously it doesn't work here. Yet, if I will then sit them down from their own cable TV and point out to them, the percentage of their TV that is nothing but infomercials dwarfs that in the United States. And I pose the question, well, are they doing, is this a community service? Are these people donating? Clearly, people in your country speaking your language are responding to direct response. And sometimes they are just badly dubbed the same commercials. They're horribly done and it still works. I think there's a larger issue where people just don't like salesmen. They don't like the idea of being sold to. And they don't like to admit that they were sold on something. And this is just part of the natural resistance that people have. I don't like being sold either. But if you step away from this in the capitalist system, the advertiser is presenting a service where if you have a problem, I can help you solve this problem. Or if you're in a situation that needs information, I have the information that can help you. Or if you need a service such as accountancy or law help, then I have that. But you need. So while in a market of 100,000 people, most of the people will not be in that receptive part of the market. They will say, I don't care about this. So this is nonsense. The small number of people that will respond, and in that small number, is a fortune. That's where fortunes are made in markets, is going after the people who you want and need and can use and will appreciate what you have. It's not everybody because you're not going after everybody. It's those people who, what we call your target audience. So like Stan was saying, the golfers understood this. The non-golfers thought, this is ridiculous. So don't worry about the naysayers. Worry about the people who are your perfect prospects. Not about the people who wouldn't buy from you no matter what you wrote or what kind of message you put up. Yeah. Another tale you told me when we first started working together, I'm sure it was just one of those fairy tales, copywriters tell other people, yet actually happened when we submitted ad copy to people they actually came back with, well, my daughter is a English major and she doesn't like this ad. And the question is, well, how many millions of dollars has your daughter sold? The phone goes silent and either this client, we convinced it to go ahead with it, but that was his national, that was the filter he was running it through that we had used slang or somewhere along the way. Well, these clients that I still am very good friends with these clients and like I said, I'm still collecting royalties on ads that are 20 years old from them because they keep changing the ads to fit whatever medium is now being used. They're on mobile devices now. The ads work because they're timeless. But before these clients came along who said, we'll run what you write, most of the clients that I had stepped on an ad that I have and they would bring their daughter with the English literature degree from college who wanted to change all the punctuation, who wanted to change a lot of the words, who insisted that any of the language I was using was incorrect and they would change it. They would do that and then I would get the blame if the ad bombed. And so it's very frustrating when you understand what needs to be done, when you understand that your audience is going to respond to slang, is going to enjoy having a rollicking story that's not conservative and maybe break some taboos, that that is what your audience wants and you know that and you have a client that won't let you do it. So that's very, very frustrating. So what we're teaching here is what works, not what will make your client happy. And like I say, every client I've had, even the ones who are making tons of money, we're always a little nervous with a lot of the materials that I wrote. Okay. All right. Another question that comes up frequently is the examples that you use mostly in the course for one, it's because you've written and two, they make good teaching examples. They tend to be what I'm going to call like their passion markets. The people who buy this like golfers, they love golf if they're buying this stuff. I'm trying, I'm doing like other examples that we've used. People who are, let's say if you're buying a weight loss product for at least that period of your life, you're interested in that as opposed to we have many people, many clients, people go to the simple writing system who are, they're selling to businesses. They don't view their product as the buyer needs this thing, it will help them, but they're not excited about this. So how do you apply the concept of hooks into what is typically they'll say it's a business to business thing where they're not going to get all excited and buy right away. You need to hook them in different ways. Well, this is where you get into the objections and countering the objections. If you have something that's going to help a businessman, if you're going business to business, remember that still human beings, they have lives, they have to make a profit, they have sales that go into the dumpster. Gary Halbert used to talk about finding the client when they're in heat. And a lot of times clients will not care about what you're offering for most of their lives or most of the year, but for a brief period of time, while the winds of misfortune are against them, they will be in heat. They may see your ad or come across your ad or know about you for years and years and years and then for a very short period of time, something happens which suddenly puts them into your target audience. Say they live in a house, they never thought they were going to move, suddenly they got to move. Now they're interested in what it takes to find a new house and go through all that. In a business, things are fine, sales are fine, suddenly new competition comes along, Walmart moves into town. Something happens and sales collapse and suddenly you're in heat. People, even businessmen who are on the executive penthouse suite with the corner office, they have regular human needs which includes panic when things go wrong, which includes fear of the competition, which includes a healthy dose of greed that if other people are starting to make money in a certain change in the market or a certain opportunity comes up, they want to get in on it too. This becomes levels of passion. Even if you're a dentist and you're just offering regular dental stuff to clients, again, when you need what they have, there is a level of passion that comes up. When you're selling, it's always an emotional based decision to buy. There's always a sense of you're reaching up that point and then you finally make that final jump. We say, yes, I'm going to get this. I'm going to change dentists and go with you. I'm going to actually trust you and hire you for your services. I'm actually going to buy your book. I'm going to buy your product. I'm going to get going. Once they do, they have made a decision that often lets them sit back and either be relieved that now they've taken a step and things are going to move and they're going to either go along with that or whatever, but things are going to change. He's taken a step towards fixing what was wrong. Even in B2B, that is the situation. These are humans with their job. Even a CEO's job is on the line with the decisions they make. Even the people, nobody is bored about what they do. I like to tell a story about, I stumbled by a seminar in Vegas with just accountants. Normally we think of accountants as humorless guys who have their ties cinched up all day long and they're dealing with numbers and they're just doing their thing. I got to tell you in Vegas, they're wild. The ties come off and they're having a blast and they're telling stories that make each other laugh uproariously. These are tales they can't tell their family. Their wife doesn't want to hear about their day at work. Their kids are embarrassed at school with their dads and accountant. They have no outlets and then you get them around other accounts and suddenly the shackles come off and they're having a lot of fun and they're telling war stories and trading stories about the worst clients that they've had and all the troubles and how late they stayed up before tax day filling out stuff. In their world, what other people may not find exciting is very exciting and very interesting to them. Actually, part of your detective work, going back up, is this is what you have to tap into to sell to these people. If you are not an accountant or don't have insight in that, then your job is to write an exciting thing. Better hang out with accountants, better find out what they think. What do they find funny? What do they find interesting? What do they like? What juice is their day? What wakes them up? Yeah, as you said. Or if it is that you really believe it's avoidance, well then you're selling them the rest of their life. Get the time back. You don't have to worry about this. No, very few people go into business for the sake of employing accountants. Most businesses, they didn't start the business so they get to have a website. So however, this is part of their, this is the thing that's bothering them. It's keeping them up at night. So that is the, you're tapping into that part. The hook is cheaper, easier, if not do greater because they might not be interested in greater. They might be interested in just, I don't want to think about this. In fact, I think we have an ad to Kyra Proctors, I think, which it'll come up, which the whole thing is not about making their business better but giving them their life back. So that's a really good point, Stan, is a lot of people work too much and the work, if you can tell them that how what you do helps them get more of their life back, more time with the family, more time off, that it's not always about more profits and more bottom line. The bottom line for humans is a better life as a human in many cases. Okay, last question for this section really has nothing to do with this section. But every time we do a training class, this question starts to leak in here. So I thought for the sake of people who are, this is hanging over your head, let's just solve this one now. And I know this comes up again later. How long does the, because you're actually writing, how long does the copy need to be? Does anybody read those things? What is the perfect length for my sales video? A lot of the ads that I've been showing have been long copy ads and that is one of the resistant points. Like in the, going back to golf, when we ran a three page ad in golf for a couple of those ads, that meant it was a whole page of copy in the magazine and you turn it, in turn the page and there were two more pages all copy. No, no photos, maybe one photo, just solid copy. It was, it was literally like reading an article or reading a book. And every other ad in the issue were all sparse. At most they'd have like a little slogan or they'd have a little bit of copy, but they, it was all about the picture. It was all about the, the, the, how beautiful the swing is or how beautiful the club looks or how wonderful the ball is or they try to use stop action stuff. So it was all about visuals with the copy being the last thing and that's the way people think about advertising. Nobody reads long ads. I will tell you after 30 years, yes they do. The, the, the reframe we like to use is the more you tell, the more you sell. However, you cannot bore people and you, and when you, when you do long copy, you don't do long copy just because long copy works. It's, it's because you start at the beginning and imagine the conversation that you're going to need to convince somebody to do what you want them to do. What do you need to say to persuade them? So you go through those stages, which are the stages of the simple writing system and when you're done, you stop and then you look at how long it was and oh, that took me, you know, several manuscript pages to, to get across. So that took me 20 minutes in a, in a, in a video or maybe it took five minutes in a video, maybe it only took half a page, maybe in a, in a short email, you can do that. You don't write long emails if a short one works. You don't write long ads if a short one will work. In my experience, to be able to persuade people to do something, to go through all the steps that you need to go through to convince them, to get over their objections, to explain things enough so they feel comfortable moving to the next step, it requires, it requires some time to, to get there. That's why long copy ads have been the standard for direct response, for entrepreneurs, for even big businesses are starting to catch on to this. That's why infomercials tend to be very, very long on TV. It's because that's what works. That's what you need to say. So do not write, do not overwrite because you think the more words I have, the better off we're going to be. It's just, think in terms of the sales message. What do you need to say to get your prospect to become a customer? What needs to go into that? And that should be your standard. Yeah, right, exactly. It needs to be long enough. That's really the answer. That was the, you explained it better. But so for right now as you're going through the simple writing system, don't limit yourself by I know I only, this is only going to be a 30 second video or it's only going to be this many column inches or the webpage can only be this big. You may have a physical constraint. You may get into postcard marketing. But for right now, going through the course, that's if not the last section, one of the last sections where we'll start to tackle that. For right now, just we build your sales message. Do not think of any physical or time constraints. There's a time for that. This is not it. Okay, by the way, just for the people who are in the coaching program, this, a lot of these questions came because when people were doing hooks and writing this, this was one of the areas where having a coach really, really helped. It was good to bounce off of a coach and go back and forth. Once you get it, it really makes sense. And it makes sense to your brain and it makes sense when you sit down to write. Once you understand the hook, it may take a couple of tries to get there. So that's, that's one of the things we're having somebody to bounce off of can really, really help. Exactly. Saves hours, weeks, years of trial and error perhaps. All right. That wraps up the Q&A for the hook section. There's, as John just mentioned, if you're in the coaching program, you'll, you'll get the exercise, a chance to post to get some feedback. And if you're not in the coaching program, obviously these exercises stand alone. You should still do them for your, your own sake or perhaps consider tacking on the coaching program at some point. Thanks. All right. Here's your exercise for step number seven, hooks. I want you to write down a list of the objections that your perfect prospect may have and how you counter them. So this gets back to sitting down and thinking about that face-to-face conversation. You would have with your perfect prospect, what do they keep coming up with where they say, I don't know, I can't afford it. It's not for me. This is way too complicated or I don't have the education. What does your perfect prospect say in his head or what would he say if you could interview him about this that is going to stop him cold from moving on to the next step? And then how do you counter that? It's not expensive. There's a payment plan. You don't need massive education for this. Most of our customers, you know, haven't finished high school. Whatever it is that you say, get those down. So do five or six objections. You don't need to go through everything possible and do five or six counters to that. But then from that, find out where the hook is. What challenges the common wisdom that your prospect has? How does what you have differ from what the competition is offering or what he believes is out there? What is different, unique? What is maybe a little odd or a little strange? So go to town on this. Have some fun with it. Because again, finding hooks is often a matter of interviewing people and finding out what those stories are, what the rumors are, what the inside story is. So this is a good place to start. Don't worry about making the hook too outrageous because you can dial it back, but start looking for hooks now. So do that and then I'll see you on the other side.
